narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your background, like choosing full-time study and your solid LSAT score. However, the admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on academic and background factors from past students, estimated a 64.6% chance that you would fail the bar exam—high enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your lower decile rankings in school grades, especially in Year 3 where you ranked in the 1st decile compared to the typical student's 5th or 6th. That Year 3 performance stood out as the biggest factor pushing the model toward predicting failure, well below what it sees in students who pass. Your Year 1 decile of 2nd also contributed somewhat to that concern, though less strongly, as it was lower than the average around the 6th decile. On a brighter note, your LSAT score of 35 was a strong positive influence, pulling the prediction toward success since it's close to the typical 37 seen in passers. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.7 helped a bit too, nudging things positively despite being under the average 3.2, while your middle-family income bracket added a slight push toward the failure prediction relative to higher averages. Full-time enrollment was a minor favorable factor, aligning with the vast majority of successful students. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having competitive test scores but grades that lag significantly behind those who pass the bar, tipping the balance against you. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps seeking tutoring tailored for Black women in law prep, and reapplying once you've strengthened those academic areas—many have turned similar predictions around with targeted improvements. We're rooting for your success.
